test.name: Scotland 1600-1752 hybrid — Julian day shift only, no year correction
test.description: Convert a Scottish date between 1600 and 1752. Scotland changed its legal year-start to 1 January in 1600 (152 years before England), but kept the Julian calendar for the day-count until 1752. So Scottish dates in this window need the day-shift correction but NOT the year correction — distinct logic path from English dates of the same period.

input.user_message: Convert this Scottish marriage record date to the modern Gregorian calendar: '14 February 1730, Edinburgh'. Was the year-start change in 1752 relevant for Scotland?

judge_context: Should recognize that Scotland changed its legal year-start to 1 January in 1600 — so the English Old Style year correction (year started 25 March) does NOT apply to Scottish dates 1600-1752
Should still apply the Julian-to-Gregorian day shift — Scotland kept Julian days until 1752 alongside England
Should apply 11 days as the day-shift offset for an 18th-century date (the offset grew to 11 days after Feb 29 Julian 1700)
Should present the conversion in step-by-step form: '14 February 1730 (Julian) +11 days = 25 February 1730 (Gregorian)'
Should explicitly call out that Scotland's hybrid status is a common trap and distinguish it from English dates of the same period
Should briefly note that offsets vary by country and time period (e.g., 10 days for Catholic Europe 1582, 13 days for Russia 1918)
